d: 4 processions through Palma old town on March 29. La Borriquita (18:00) is most family-friendly. Hooded penitents, ornate floats, brass bands through candlelit sandstone streets. Mallorca's 33 cofradías each have distinct robes.
dt: Your final day — don't miss it. Position near Plaça de Cort or Passeig del Born.

d: Free 3-day music festival at Parc de la Mar beneath Palma Cathedral. Mar 27–29. Multiple stages, local and Spanish acts. Marks the official start of tourist season.
dt: Cathedral as backdrop. Vermut Day on Sunday.
sq: Parc de la Mar Palma cathedral

d: 21st edition. 15+ galleries across Palma, Andratx, Pollença, Santanyí open simultaneously with free entry and refreshments. March 21, 11:00–15:00.
dt: Guided gallery tours run all week after.

d: 33rd edition, March 22. 10K at 09:00, half marathon at 10:00 from Parc de la Mar. Flat scenic route past Bellver Castle.
dt: Bib collection at El Corte Inglés, Fri 16–21, Sat 10–20.

d: 55th edition. 800+ teams, 1,200+ sailors from 62 nations in all 10 Olympic classes. March 27–April 4. Free waterfront spectating.
dt: First event of 2026 Sailing Grand Slam.

d: Palma's annual funfair. 200+ attractions including 60m Ferris wheel. Dating to 18th century. Open throughout your stay.
dt: Weekdays 16:30–23:00, weekends from 11:00.

d: One of Mallorca's oldest markets with a live animal section — unique on the island. 08:00–13:00.
dt: Take the vintage train from Inca.

d: Mallorca's largest market — ~500 stalls famous for leather goods.
dt: 5 min from Binissalem wine country.

d: Centenary of Gaudí's death. Year-long programme at Palma Cathedral — his only major work outside Catalonia. Exhibition 'Gaudí at the Seu', special guided tours.
dt: Cathedral entry ~€9. The baldachin is extraordinary.

d: Talaia d'Alcúdia on the La Victòria peninsula is a top pick when you want proximity and real drama: the full circuit climbs to a 446 m summit with 360° views over both bays, drops to the remote Platja des Coll Baix—pale pebbles beneath sheer wooded cliffs, reachable only on foot or by boat—and returns through forest. That loop is about 12 km, 4–5 hours, with 670 m gain and moderate-hard effort; a shorter summit-only route is 7.4 km in 2–3 hours. On clear days Menorca is visible on the horizon. On the same peninsula, Penya Rotja adds a detour to a 354 m viewpoint with a 400-year-old iron cannon from a 1630 guard post and an access passage cut through the rock. The route follows an exposed ridge with steep drops and a cliff-edge section with a wooden handrail—avoid it in strong wind or wet conditions.
dt: Choose the full circuit (12 km) for the beach, or the shorter summit loop (7.4 km) if time is tight. Skip in wind or rain: exposed ridge and cliff-edge drops. Add Penya Rotja for the cannon and rock passage.

d: Sa Cova Tancada is the standout free cave on Mallorca: a narrow corridor opens into a vast cavity where stalactite and stalagmite formations rival commercial show caves, with some columns topping ten metres. You can explore roughly 250 metres of chambers, but reaching them means tackling the final stretch of descent—about 200 metres of steep ground with fixed chains where you will need your hands and steady nerves, so it is not a choice for anyone uneasy with heights. Bring a proper headlamp; there is no substitute underground. The drive from Sa Pobla takes about twenty minutes, and unlike ticketed caves, access costs nothing.
dt: Pair it with the Talaia d'Alcúdia circuit for a full day out; pack gloves if you like extra grip on the chains.

d: Fonts Ufanes is Mallorca's must-see hydrogeological phenomenon: after sustained heavy rain in the Tramuntana, water surges up through the forest floor from a karst aquifer, creating sudden rivers and waterfalls across an ancient holm oak woodland. Rainwater infiltrates the Puig Tomir limestone massif, fills a permeable aquifer ringed by impervious clay, and when capacity is exceeded, water forces itself through the soil. The springs typically run October–March, often 24–48 hours after heavy rainfall; the site has been a Natural Monument since 2001. Even when the springs are dry, the woodland is enchanting. A 3.2 km loop through the Finca Gabellí Petit estate takes under an hour; entry is free and the site is open daily 10:00–17:00.
dt: Rain is the show—visit after Tramuntana downpours; waterproof shoes and a mini-tripod help for long exposures. If the springs are quiet, pair with the nearby commercial Coves de Campanet (~€20).
sq: Fonts Ufanes Campanet Mallorca water forest

d: Puig de Maria rises steeply above Pollença to a 14th-century monastery at 330 m, where a full 360° panorama opens over both bays, the Formentor peninsula, and the Tramuntana range. The monastery café serves traditional Mallorcan food, a welcome reward after the climb. The round trip is about 4.4 km with roughly 300 m of gain and typically takes 1.5–2 hours on easy-to-moderate paths. For the effort, few walks on the island deliver such a dramatic viewpoint.
dt: Confirm monastery and café hours before you go; late afternoon light over the bays is especially good from up top.

d: Vall de Bóquer (Boquer Valley) is an easy outing from Port de Pollença: roughly 5 km along a valley lined with dramatic rock pinnacles, rosemary, and dwarf fan palms to a remote pebble beach, with wild goats often grazing along the way. Allow about 1.5–2 hours for the round trip; the gain is only around 150 m and the walking is easy, but the trail is not sheltered, so it is best avoided in wind or heavy rain. At Coll de Moro, take the right fork to reach a cliff-edge viewpoint for dramatic coastal views and photography rather than heading straight down to the beach.
dt: Fork right at Coll de Moro for the cliff viewpoint; the valley is exposed—skip in wind or rain.

d: S'Albufera is the Balearics' largest wetland—over 1,650 hectares—and sits practically at Sa Pobla's doorstep, with more than 303 recorded bird species. Four marked routes from 0.7 to 11.5 km thread along reed-lined canals to eight observation hides. Late March is prime time, when spring migration brings ospreys, purple herons, marsh harriers, glossy ibis, and rare warblers. Entry is free; collect a permit at the visitor centre (open 9:00–17:00). Flat paths and hides offer shelter, so a visit still works in light rain.
dt: Dawn and dusk give the best light for photography. Pick up your free permit at the visitor centre before you walk the routes.

d: The Es Camell loop near Lluc Monastery threads through forest canopy past a fantastically eroded karst outcrop shaped like a camel, the Bronze Age burial cave Sa Cometa des Morts, ancient charcoal-making sites (sitges), and a 500-year-old holm oak. It is a moderate outing of about 5.5 km with roughly 165 m of gain, usually 1.5–2 hours on the trail. Sa Cometa des Morts is a Bronze and Iron Age burial cave from the 4th century BC, reachable on the route; it descends at an angle about 60 m into the mountain and includes a short crawling passage around 30 m deep, and excavated skeletal remains are on display in the Lluc Museum—bring a headlamp. The hike is a solid pick in bad weather, when the woods give shelter and you can warm up at the monastery restaurant or visit its museum afterward.
dt: Headlamp essential for Sa Cometa: steep descent (~60 m) and a crawl section near 30 m. Pair the walk with the Lluc Museum to see finds from the cave. Forest cover and monastery facilities make this workable in wet weather.

d: Puig de Massanella is Mallorca's second-highest peak but the highest you can climb: Puig Major's summit is off limits as a military zone. The usual route from Lluc runs through Finca Comafreda (€6 at the booth)—about 15.5 km round trip, 5–6 hours, roughly 840 m of gain, and hard going. Near the summit the karst limestone plateau is striking, with razor-sharp erosion, deep sinkholes, and on clear days views across to Menorca and mainland Catalonia; historic snow houses (cases de neu) punctuate the upper slopes. In March, snow and ice can still sit on the summit area, and cairn navigation near the top is treacherous in fog, so a clear day is best. A free alternative from the Cúber reservoir via Coll des Prat skips the Comafreda fee but is longer and trickier to follow.
dt: Choose a clear day—fog makes cairn navigation near the top very hard. Expect possible snow/ice on the summit in colder months. To avoid the €6 fee, try Cúber via Coll des Prat (longer, harder to navigate).

d: Mirador Es Colomer at Cap de Formentor is Mallorca's most famous viewpoint: built platforms and about 130 steps descend to a perch over 300-metre vertical cliffs, the rocky Es Colomer islet, and the ragged north coast. No long hike is required—access is roadside. Summer car restrictions on the Formentor road run 15 May–15 October; in late March the road is fully open. Arrive early for soft light and before parking fills. Just above, Talaia d'Albercutx watchtower, a 16th-century pirate lookout at 380 m, is one of the best sunrise spots on the island, with east-facing views over Formentor beach, the headland, and Menorca on clear days—a roughly three-mile out-and-back walk or partial drive.
dt: Early morning for soft light and easier parking; expect May–Oct car limits on the road. For sunrise, walk or partly drive to Talaia d'Albercutx above the mirador.
sq: Mirador Es Colomer Formentor Mallorca cliff

d: The 365 Calvari Steps in Pollença—traditionally one cypress-lined step for each day of the year—climb to a small Baroque chapel with rooftop views over the terracotta town below. Allow roughly half an hour for the round trip; the walk is free and still rewarding in light rain, when the stone steps can feel especially atmospheric. A guitarist often plays near the top, adding to the mood. On Palm Sunday (29 March), Pollença’s most important annual religious event unfolds here as the Via Crucis procession descends these same steps.
dt: Free and quick (~30 min round trip); still worth it in light rain. Listen for a guitarist at the chapel. Palm Sunday’s Via Crucis draws big crowds—arrive early or pick another day for a quieter climb.

d: The Torrent de Pareis is the largest karstic canyon in the Balearic Islands, with 250–300 m limestone walls that narrow to an arm's width at Sa Fosca. The full traverse runs from Escorca (about 630 m) through S'Entreforc down to Sa Calobra beach — roughly 6.5 km, three to five hours, about 600 m of descent, and rated hard, with house-sized boulder scrambles, cold pools that require wading, and stretches of total darkness. The gorge holds caves including the Cova del Soldat Pelut and travertine deposits. After rain—especially in March and early spring—flash flood risk is serious; check forecasts for several dry days before attempting, and expect cold water in the pools. For dramatic scenery without the full descent, you can walk in from Sa Calobra beach through hand-carved pedestrian tunnels. No permit is required; plan transport carefully because bus service to Escorca usually does not begin until April, so two vehicles or a pre-arranged taxi are often needed for a one-way hike.
dt: Flash floods after rain are life-threatening—do not enter during or right after wet weather. Buses to Escorca start around April; otherwise use two cars or book a taxi. The road to Sa Calobra has 26 hairpins.
sq: Torrent de Pareis Sa Calobra canyon Mallorca

d: The Barranc de Biniaraix is one of Mallorca's most beautiful hikes, ascending a gorge on some 1,932 cobblestone steps of Arab or Roman origin that are declared a Site of Cultural Interest. From the hamlet of Biniaraix (pop. 124) the trail climbs through terraced olive groves to the spectacular narrow section at S'Estret, where the Mirador de Xim Quesada offers a pulpit-shaped viewpoint over the gorge. The classic circuit is about 8 km with roughly 400 m of gain and two to three hours for the gorge section at a moderate pace; continuing to Cornador Gran (956 m) turns it into a much bigger day with about 1,150 m of climbing. In March conditions are excellent: water flows through the torrent, vegetation is green, and wildflowers are starting. Parking is restricted on weekends, so many walkers leave the car in Sóller and walk about 1.5 km to the trailhead; the bodega on Biniaraix village square serves drinks year-round.
dt: Weekends: park in Sóller and walk ~1.5 km to the start. The lower gorge is sheltered; refreshments at the village square bodega.
sq: Barranc Biniaraix stone steps gorge Mallorca

d: A castle has occupied this dramatic cliff-top site since 902 AD, with two extraordinary viewpoints: the Orengar panorama from Puig Major toward Puig de Massanella, and a southern vista over the Bay of Palma toward Cabrera. From Orient the walk is about 8.5 kilometres with roughly 590 metres of gain—moderate to challenging—through idyllic olive groves where sheep graze, quieter than the approach from Alaró; from the Es Verger restaurant the outing is often about two to three hours on the trail. At the summit, a refuge serves coffee and cake (cash only; supplies arrive by donkey). At the base, the legendary Es Verger is the place for roast lamb shoulder (paletilla de cordero). In March the weather is often ideal—cool and comfortable—with fewer crowds.
dt: Bring cash for the summit café. Time a meal at Es Verger for paletilla de cordero after the descent.

d: The Camí de s'Arxiduc is a ridgeline path engineered by Archduke Ludwig Salvator above Valldemossa, and it ranks among the Mediterranean's most spectacular walks—typically 11–17 km depending on the variant, 4–5 hours, with roughly 600–750 m of ascent and moderate-to-hard difficulty. The Mirador de Ses Puntes, a jagged stone viewpoint the Archduke built on sheer cliffs, is the star of the route, with views across the Deià coastline, Sa Foradada, and Sóller Bay. From Puig Gros (938 m) you get sweeping 360° panoramas. The Muntanya del Voltor section requires a daily permit booked via info@muntanyadelvoltor.com; the Camí de ses Basses alternative bypasses that stretch yet still reaches the ridge. The path is fully exposed, with sheer drops, and is extremely dangerous in high winds—do not attempt it in stormy or gusty conditions.
dt: Book the Voltor permit at info@muntanyadelvoltor.com or take Camí de ses Basses to skip that section. Avoid the ridge entirely in high wind.

d: La Trapa is a ruined Trappist monastery founded in 1810 by monks fleeing the French Revolution, set on a high ridge above dramatic Mediterranean cliffs where the dragon-shaped island of Sa Dragonera rises from the sea. The classic loop from Sant Elm is about 9.5 km, takes roughly three to four hours, and gains around 500 m of elevation—moderate to hard overall—with rewarding open views along the way. Expect a short chain-assisted scramble that needs steady footing and a head for exposure. A detour to Torre Cala Basset, a climbable four-hundred-year-old pirate watchtower, adds an extra shot of drama to the walk.
dt: Sturdy gloves help on the chain section; add Torre Cala Basset for a memorable pirate-watchtower lookout over the coast.
sq: La Trapa Sant Elm Dragonera Mallorca

d: The classic reservoir circuit is an easy, gentle walk (**4 km, 1–1.5 hours**) around milky-blue water with Puig Major (1,445 m) rising behind you. Scan the sky for ospreys, red kites, and griffon vultures; in March the reservoirs are usually full and at their most photogenic. Stepping up the effort, Puig de l'Ofre (1,093 m) can be tackled from Cúber (**10 km roundtrip, 4–5 hours, ~300 m gain**). The **Tossals Verds circuit** (**12 km, 4–5 hours, ~480 m gain, moderate–hard**) loops from Cúber to the mountain refuge through gorges and over passes, including a 270-year-old aqueduct and chain-assisted scramble sections; Refugi de Tossals Verds serves meals and has a bar with a fireplace.
dt: Start early for Puig de l'Ofre or Tossals Verds and check refugi opening hours if you want a meal or a drink by the fire after the scrambles.

d: Seven surviving prehistoric burial caves from the Middle Bronze Age (~1600 BC) lie freely accessible within the residential area of Cala Sant Vicenç, reached after a short stroll through a beautiful holm oak forest. Oval and rectangular chambers open into corridors, benches, and niches; one cave may date to 3500 BC. The visit is very easy and suitable for children, with about a 25-minute drive and no entry fee. Nearby, Coves Blanques can be reached along the Camí dels Presos (Prisoners' Path), built 1937–1940 by Republican prisoners from a Civil War concentration camp—there, whitish stalactites and fossil remains of the extinct endemic goat-like mammal Myotragus balearicus reward a roughly 35-minute walk through pine forest with panoramic views of the Cavall Bernat cliffs.
dt: Pair with Coves Blanques via the Camí dels Presos for stalactites, Myotragus fossils, and Cavall Bernat views—allow time for the longer forest walk.
sq: Alzinaret Cala Sant Vicenc caves prehistoric

d: These medieval quarry caves are hollowed from sandstone sea cliffs; the stone quarried here helped build Palma Cathedral. Three enormous rectangular openings lead into chambers roughly eighty metres long, where a carved altar and religious inscriptions survive from shipwrecked Genoese sailors. It is only about a ten-minute walk from Portals Vells beach, and daylight reaches deep inside, so you can explore without a torch. The site is family-friendly and free to visit.
dt: Walk from Portals Vells beach in ~10 min; daylight fills the chambers, so leave the torch at home.

d: 22–25m stalagmite — among tallest in Europe. Entrance in sea cliffs 50m above Mediterranean. €15.
dt: Evidence sea was 16m higher 3.3 million years ago.

d: Lake Martel — one of Europe's largest underground lakes. 177m long. Classical concerts from boats. €17.
dt: Book online to avoid queues.
sq: Cuevas Drach Porto Cristo underground lake

d: Es Pontàs is a natural sea arch about twenty metres high near Santanyí, carved by marine erosion from Miocene reef limestone. From free parking nearby, a walk of roughly fifteen minutes leads to a viewpoint with benches overlooking the arch and the sea. The formation is legendary in climbing as the site of Chris Sharma's deep-water solo ascent, rated 9a+, which helped establish psicobloc in the wider imagination. For visitors who are not climbing, sunrise and sunset are the most rewarding times to visit, when the light plays across the rock and water.
dt: Use the free parking and allow ~15 minutes to the bench viewpoint; aim for sunrise or sunset for the best light.
sq: Es Pontas sea arch Mallorca Santanyi

d: Sa Foradada is widely regarded as Mallorca’s most iconic sunset viewpoint: a rocky peninsula where an 18-metre hole in the cliff—the foradada, meaning “pierced”—frames the sun as it drops toward the sea. The classic photo is from the roadside viewpoint at the Son Marroig estate; a trail of roughly 45 minutes descends to the peninsula itself if you want to stand beneath the arch. Parking is tight and fills early, so plan to arrive well ahead of sunset. The Son Marroig museum, the former estate of Archduke Ludwig Salvator, adds context and a marble rotunda that looks straight out over the water.
dt: Arrive well before sunset—parking fills fast. Visit the Son Marroig museum’s marble rotunda for sea views before you take the roadside viewpoint.

d: The Salt des Freu waterfall near Orient tumbles as two 10-metre cascades into emerald pools, a compact highlight that still feels wild when the water is up. Flow is strongest from October through April, with March often offering the best mix of reliable cascades and pleasant walking weather. The short route is about 2.6 km and roughly an hour on easy-to-moderate terrain—ideal if you want the falls without a major hike. After rain, especially on weekends, it draws crowds; plan an early start if you want space around the pools.
dt: On weekends after rain, aim to arrive before 11 AM—parking and the pools fill up fast once word spreads.
sq: Salt des Freu waterfall Orient Mallorca

d: The Sa Calobra road (Ma-2141) was designed by engineer Antonio Parietti and built entirely by hand over six years before it opened in 1932. It drops about 800 meters through 26 hairpin bends carved into exposed Jurassic limestone. At km 5, the Nus de sa Corbata (the "tie knot") executes a 270° turn where the road passes over itself and then under itself; this is the only section that needs support pillars. The drive is one of Mallorca's great showcases of early 20th-century road engineering.
dt: Combine with Torrent de Pareis; parking at Sa Calobra is free in March.

d: This 395-hectare public estate between Can Picafort and Son Serra de Marina is home to the Necropolis of Son Real, the Balearics' largest prehistoric cemetery (7th century BC), with monumental stone tombs set right on the waterline. Walking trails lead through scented pine forests and dunes, past a pre-Talayotic settlement and a medieval farm with live animals, and a free interpretation centre helps make sense of the landscape. Typical routes run about 6–8.5 km in 1.5–2.5 hours—easy and flat—yet most tourists who stay in Can Picafort never venture this far. Entry and parking are free, and the drive from the resort strip is only about 15 minutes. In rough weather the pine canopy shelters much of the path, though the dunes and shore stay more exposed.
dt: Bring sun and wind protection for the open dunes and waterline; under the pines the walk stays pleasant even when the coast feels harsh.
sq: Necropolis Son Real Mallorca coast tombs

d: Atmospheric walled talayotic enclosure engulfed by massive ancient oaks. Central talayot. ~€4.
dt: Unique Balearic megalithic culture.

d: Medieval Jewish quarter. Monti-Sion church built atop synagogue. 37 burned alive in 1691. Walking tours available.
dt: One of Europe's most extraordinary crypto-Jewish stories.

d: 160+ machine-gun nests built 1939–42 by POW slave labour. Concrete bunkers on pristine white sand. Some painted with poetry.
dt: Also at Can Picafort (15 min from Sa Pobla).
sq: Es Trenc bunker Civil War Mallorca beach

d: Most important Roman site on Mallorca, founded 123 BC. Forum, residential quarter, theatre. ~€4.
dt: Combine with Alcúdia market on Sundays.

d: Building by Hassan Fathy. 150+ children's portraits in underground cistern. Woolly rhinoceros + Swarovski curtain. Sculpture park, camera obscura. ~€12, free Tuesdays.
dt: Most extraordinary hidden cultural site.
sq: Sa Bassa Blanca museum Alcudia Mallorca

d: Only circular castle in Spain, one of 3–4 in Europe. Built 1300–1311, Gothic. €4, free Sundays.
dt: Panoramic views of Palma and the bay.

d: World's largest original Gothic rose window (13.8m). Gaudí's baldachin. Barceló's ceramic seascape mural. ~€9.
dt: 2026 is Gaudí Year — centenary exhibitions.
sq: Palma Cathedral La Seu interior Mallorca

d: Miró's studio preserved as he left it in 1983 — paint splashes, unfinished canvases. Sculpture garden.
dt: Combined ticket with Es Baluard available.

d: Since 1912 in unchanged wooden carriages through 13 tunnels. Connect with tram to Port de Sóller. ~€25 return.
dt: Atlas Obscura featured. Departs Palma.

d: Founded 1700 — among Spain's oldest cafés. Originally sold mountain snow/ice. 18th-century décor intact. Ensaimadas + hot chocolate.
dt: Original at Carrer de Can Sanç.
sq: Can Joan de s'Aigo cafe Palma Mallorca

d: Palma's main food market. Mon–Sat 07–15. Stall cooks fish you buy. Tapas bars inside. Saturday mornings liveliest.
dt: Combine with Santa Catalina market.

d: 400-year-old Sóller estate. Daily English tour 12:00 (~€25). Oil, bread, ramallet tomatoes, almonds, oranges.
dt: Combine with Sóller market + vintage tram.

d: Unfinished church by Gaudí student Joan Rubió. Spectacular roofless Gothic/Modernist arches open to sky. Free.
dt: Blue sky framed by stone arches — extraordinary photos.
sq: Esglesia Nova Son Servera roofless church Mallorca

n: Drac de Na Coca — Embalmed Dragon
d: Embalmed 'dragon' (crocodile) in Diocesan Museum. 17th-century folklore: monster in Palma sewers. Fire-breathing effigy parades every February.
dt: Sculpture on facade in Plaza Santa Eulalia.
sq: Drac Na Coca dragon Palma Mallorca

d: Discovered as fossils 1977, assumed extinct 2,000 years. Living specimens found 1980. Only 3.5cm, nowhere else on Earth. ~500 breeding pairs.
dt: Attenborough came to film it. Sóller museum has exhibits.

d: Extinct goat-antelope. World's first mammal with reptilian bone growth — cold-blooded characteristics. Forward-facing eyes, single incisor. Extinct ~3000 BC.
dt: Manacor History Museum and Sóller Natural Science Museum.

d: Historic cases de neu—dry-stone snow houses—dot the upper slopes of Puig de Massanella, with eleven structures on peaks above 1,000 m built from the 16th to the 19th centuries. Workers packed winter snow into the buildings, insulated it with vegetation, and sent it down by mule for town refrigeration, a trade that fed Palma’s ice culture and traditions such as Ca'n Joan de s'Aigo (founded 1700), which began selling mountain snow and ice. They appear along the standard Massanella route from Lluc; many stand on a karst limestone plateau near the summit where the landscape feels otherworldly, carved by razor-sharp erosion and deep sinkholes.
dt: Take the standard Massanella hike from Lluc (~5 hrs round trip) and watch for the snow houses on the upper slopes and the karst plateau below the summit.
sq: Cases de Neu snow house Mallorca Tramuntana

d: Abandoned railway viaduct — 7 arches, 20m high, 78m long. From dismantled 1964 train line, concealed in forest. Free.
dt: Atlas Obscura featured.
sq: Puente Siete Bocas viaduct Mallorca abandoned

d: Tiny cove near Deià where mountain water creates natural clay pools. Bathers cover in clay before swimming. 30-min walk.
dt: Free, no facilities. Spectacular coastal scenery.

d: Daily master glassblowing using 300-year-old techniques. Glass Museum upstairs. Free entry, Algaida.
dt: Techniques unchanged for centuries.

d: Living museum of traditional Mallorcan life. Wed/Fri: demonstrations in traditional dress + free tastings. Thu: folk dancing. ~€15.
dt: Most immersive traditional Mallorcan experience.

n: Torre des Verger — Tower of Souls
d: Torre de ses Ànimes, also called Torre del Verger or Torre des Verger, is a 16th-century watchtower near Banyalbufar that you can enter for one of the island’s classic postcard views: UNESCO-listed agricultural terraces stepping down to the sea. The slopes around Banyalbufar still show ancient Moorish terrace farming and irrigation. The tower sits right on the cliff with steep, unprotected drops, so stay back from the edge. Late afternoon and sunset bring the warmest light on stone and terraces.
dt: Go late afternoon or at sunset for the best light. Watch your footing—the cliff drops are unprotected. Pair with a stroll in Banyalbufar to see the Moorish terraces up close.
sq: Torre des Verger Banyalbufar Mallorca watchtower
